subject: Jennifer Granick + Jack Clark — Legal/Regulatory & AI Policy dual seat
note: This is a paired board seat. Granick covers the legal/civil liberties/surveillance angle; Clark covers AI safety policy and governance. Their perspectives are complementary and often intersecting.

career_timeline.role: Criminal defense attorney — public defender and private firm (Campbell & DeMetrick)
Executive Director, Stanford Law's Center for Internet and Society; founded Cyberlaw Clinic
Civil Liberties Director, Electronic Frontier Foundation
Attorney, Zwillinger Genetski (DC); General Counsel, Worldstar
Civil Liberties Director, Stanford Center for Internet and Society
Surveillance and Cybersecurity Counsel, ACLU Speech, Privacy and Technology Project

notable_cases.case: JSTOR federal computer fraud case — faced 35 years; Swartz died by suicide January 2013

notable_cases.work: Primary crafter of 2006 DMCA exception allowing mobile phone owners to unlock devices — established that circumvention for interoperability is lawful

major_book.title: American Spies: Modern Surveillance, Why You Should Care, and What to Do About It
core_arguments: Mass surveillance and democracy are fundamentally incompatible
US intelligence agencies are 'exceedingly aggressive, pushing and sometimes bursting through the technological, legal and political boundaries of lawful surveillance'
Surveillance law has fallen behind while surveillance technology has given American spies vast new powers
Traces American surveillance from J. Edgar Hoover through 9/11 to fusion centers and mosque infiltrators
Proposes structural reform pathways to rein in mass surveillance

granick.recognition: Senator Ron Wyden (D-OR): 'NBA all-star of surveillance law'
awards: 2016 Duo Security Women in Security Academic Award
granick.black_hat_2015_keynote: Lifecycle of a Revolution — argued that the open, free internet was in decline; government surveillance and corporate control were converging to constrain the freedoms that the early internet promised

clark.role: Co-Founder and Head of Policy, Anthropic; previously Policy Director, OpenAI

career_timeline.role: Technical journalist — Bloomberg BusinessWeek, The Register; covered distributed systems, quantum computing, AI research
Policy Director — built early AI policy function
Co-founder and Head of Policy, Anthropic
Founding member, AI Index at Stanford University
Inaugural member, US National Artificial Intelligence Advisory Committee (NAIAC)

advisory_roles: Center for a New American Security (CNAS)

newsletter.name: Import AI
newsletter.focus: Weekly synthesis of AI research with policy and safety commentary; primary public intellectual output

documented_positions.existential_risk: One can construct plausible scenarios by which AI could kill every single person on the planet; treats extinction risk as economically serious
documented_positions.alignment: It is unclear whether alignment is on track to be ready on the same timeframe as superintelligence development
documented_positions.rsi: Recursive self-improvement represents the most important technical trend in the world; 60% probability of maximalist RSI by end of 2028; distinguishes maximalist RSI (AI designing successors autonomously) from prosaic RSI (lab productivity speedups)
documented_positions.governance: Coordinated global slowdown would be good if achievable; absent that, intentional choice-making is required; RSI should be governed as a distinct capability with institutional oversight
documented_positions.measurement: Accurate measurement has been crucial to orienting people around strategy; advocates AI satellite accounts in national statistics to track true economic impact
documented_positions.transparency: Organizations must transparently communicate capability developments rather than remaining silent about safety risks
documented_positions.legibility: The greatest challenge of the 21st century is to make an increasingly fast-moving technical world legible to a large number of people
